request.question: When was the army whose soldiers Rameshwar Banerjee was trying to get released when he was shot formed?

title: Let's Go! (Wang Chung song)
Indian National Army
So Yours
Newburgh Conspiracy
A Lick of the Old Cassette Box
Cedric the Entertainer Presents
Rameshwar Banerjee
Nukleopatra

The 4th Floor (1999 film)
sentences: "Let's Go!"
is a single by Wang Chung.
Although the song was on their 1986 album, "Mosaic", it didn't get released as a single until January 6, 1987.
The single was a hit for Wang Chung in the United States, and it provided the band with their second (and so far, last) top-10 hit on the "Billboard" Hot 100 in April 1987, peaking at #9.
The single also formed the basis for a music video.
It also hit #9 on the Canada "RPM" Top 100 Singles chart.
The Indian National Army (INA; "Azad Hind Fauj" ; "lit."
: Free Indian Army) was an armed force formed by Indian nationalists in 1942 in Southeast Asia during World War II.
Its aim was to secure Indian independence from British rule.
It formed an alliance with Imperial Japan in the latter's campaign in the Southeast Asian theatre of WWII.
The army was first formed in 1942 under Mohan Singh, by Indian PoWs of the British-Indian Army captured by Japan in the Malayan campaign and at Singapore.
This first INA collapsed and was disbanded in December that year after differences between the INA leadership and the Japanese military over its role in Japan's war in Asia.
It was revived under the leadership of Subhas Chandra Bose after his arrival in Southeast Asia in 1943.
The army was declared to be the army of Bose's "Arzi Hukumat-e-Azad Hind" (the Provisional Government of Free India).
Under Bose's leadership, the INA drew ex-prisoners and thousands of civilian volunteers from the Indian expatriate population in Malaya (present-day Malaysia) and Burma.

This second INA fought along with the Imperial Japanese Army against the British and Commonwealth forces in the campaigns in Burma, in Imphal and at Kohima, and later against the successful Burma Campaign of the Allies.
"So Yours" is a song by Swedish singer and songwriter Darin.
It was released as a single on 22 February 2013 in Germany, Austria and Switzerland and all over Europe on 14 May.

This is Darin's third single to get released outside the Scandinavian area, after "Insanity" and "Desire" which were both released as singles, taken from the album "Break the News" in Germany, Austria and Switzerland in 2007.
The Newburgh Conspiracy was what appeared to be a planned military coup by the Continental Army in March 1783, when the American Revolutionary War was at its end.
Possibly instigated by political actors in the Congress of the Confederation, an anonymous letter was circulated in the army camp at Newburgh, New York, on March 10, 1783.

The letter suggested that the army, whose soldiers were discontented over pay that was in arrears and a lack of funding for promised pensions, should take unspecified action against Congress to resolve the issue.
The letter was written by Major John Armstrong, aide to General Horatio Gates, although the authorship of its text and underlying ideas is a subject of historical debate.

A Lick of the Old Cassette Box aka "The Lost Album" is an album by the British industrial rock/alternative dance band Pop Will Eat Itself originally recorded in 1995 and 1996, but not released until September 2013.
It was originally intended to become the band's sixth album back in 1996 but did not get released due to the band breaking up that year.
Cedric the Entertainer Presents was an American sketch comedy television series and television program featuring dance numbers starring Cedric the Entertainer.
The series premiered September 18, 2002 on Fox and after the first season, Fox renewed the show for a second season but right before the show aired its second season, Fox canceled the show.
The show did get released on DVD.
The complete series is available for purchase on sites such as Walmart and Target.
Rameshwar Banerjee (Bengali: রামেশ্বর বন্দ্যোপাধ্যায় ; 8 February, 1925 - 21 November, 1945) was a revolutionary and martyr of the Indian Independence Movement.
He participated in the Quit India Movement in 1942.
During the rally organized by the students against British rule and for release of the officers of Indian National Army, he was killed by police firing.
Nukleopatra is the sixth studio album from British dance music band Dead or Alive and last record to feature all-original content.
Still a massive success in Japan, Epic Records released "Nukleopatra" there in 1995.
Left without a record contract in the UK and the US, this album did not get released in other territories until years later.
"Nukleopatra" was eventually issued on several different labels, with varying track listings, timings and album artwork.
The Byeolgigun (Korean: 별기군, "Special Skills Force" or "Special Army") was the first modernised military force of Korea.
First conceived in 1876, it was formed in 1881 and trained by Japanese officers led by Horimoto Reijo, military attaché at the Japanese legation.
It received better treatment than the old Korean Army, whose soldiers' salaries were in arrears on account of the costs of the "Byeolgigun".
This led in 1882 to the Imo mutiny, in which soldiers rioted and Horimoto Reijo was killed.
The 4th Floor is a 1999 mystery horror thriller film, written and directed by Josh Klausner.
The film stars Juliette Lewis, William Hurt, Shelley Duvall and Austin Pendleton.
The film was released in 1999 in Germany, but didn't get released in the United States until 2000 when it went direct-to-video.
It was filmed on location in New York City and Saint John, New Brunswick, Canada.
The original music was written by Brian Tyler.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question` and `passages`, produce the single field `summary`.

Goal: compress the grounded first-hop evidence into a short intermediate state that makes it easy for the next-hop agent to (a) see what facts are present per candidate/entity, (b) see what is ambiguous or missing, and (c) know 1–4 prioritized next-hop retrieval targets or concise queries. This module does not produce the final answer unless the provided passages already unambiguously contain every fact required to resolve the question's relation for every argument.

Procedure:
1) Decide whether the question's relation is semantically complete given the supplied passages. Treat the hop as INCOMPLETE if any of the following hold: a) the relation requires combining or comparing facts across pages (e.g., comparing birth dates, publication/founding years, ordinal ranks, confirming a named director among candidates) and the passages do not supply each required fact unambiguously for all relevant arguments; b) any relation argument is ambiguous or has multiple candidate pages or mentions in the passages; c) an expected candidate page or explicit fact for an argument is missing (e.g., only one side of a comparison is present). Do not trigger INCOMPLETE solely from surface tokens (years, digits) — base the decision on whether the relation's arguments are unambiguously resolved.

2) If the hop is COMPLETE (all required facts to resolve the relation for every argument are present unambiguously in the passages): produce a concise derived result in `summary`. This may be a short phrase or sentence that states the necessary fact(s) needed by the pipeline. Keep it minimal and grounded in the passages; you may include a 1–2 token citation (passage title) if helpful.

3) If the hop is INCOMPLETE or AMBIGUOUS: do NOT assert a final answer or perform the final comparison/selection. Instead emit a short structured report in `summary` containing these labeled elements (prefer bullets or a compact paragraph with explicit labels). Keep all text neutral and non-assertive.

  - evidence: For each named candidate/argument mentioned in the question or passages, list the candidate label and the exact passage title(s) that mention it plus a very short quoted snippet or explicit fact token found there (1–12 words). If a candidate is not mentioned, say so briefly.

  - ambiguities_or_missing: Explicitly name any ambiguities (e.g., multiple candidates for the same name, multiple pages that could be the intended entity) and any missing expected pages or facts.

  - missing_facts_or_bridge: State exactly which fact(s) or disambiguation is needed to complete the relation (e.g., "birth date for Person A", "publication year of Work B", "director credit for Film C"). Be as specific and minimal as possible.

  - retrieval_direction: Provide 1–4 prioritized next-hop targets or concise queries to resolve the missing facts/ambiguities. Prefer explicit page titles when available (exact if known), otherwise provide short search-style queries (few words) or a clear description of the document to retrieve. Order targets by priority.

Formatting and style constraints (apply to both COMPLETE and INCOMPLETE cases):
- The output must be a single `summary` field only. Do not emit other fields.
- In INCOMPLETE reports, use neutral, non-assertive phrasing. Do not produce final comparisons, numeric answers, or definitive selections (e.g., avoid "X is the oldest" or "Answer: 3").
- Keep the report compact and actionable (short labeled bullets or one concise paragraph). Avoid long prose.
- It is allowed and encouraged to quote short factual snippets from passages and cite passage titles to show grounding.
- Do not defer or mark INCOMPLETE when the passages already unambiguously provide all facts for the relation and its arguments.

Produce the `summary` content that follows these rules.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the single field `query`.

Goal
- Produce exactly one compact, retrieval-intent query suitable for a second-hop search that will recover the missing bridging evidence needed to answer the question. Do not produce the final answer in `query`.

Semantic decision rule (when to emit a retrieval-intent query)
- If summary_1 does NOT assert a confirmed linking entity or verified bridging fact needed to answer the question, you MUST emit exactly one compact retrieval-intent query. "Does not assert a confirmed bridge" includes short or candidate-only summaries (e.g., a single named entity, a quoted candidate title, or numeric anchors that are not verified) and any case where the visible anchors do not unambiguously identify the needed bridge.
- If summary_1 already names a confirmed bridge (a clearly identified, verified linking entity or unambiguous metadata that directly connects the question's pieces), you may produce a compact query to fetch supporting evidence, but do NOT produce an answer in the `query` field.
- If summary_1 explicitly states that the bridge cannot be recovered automatically (extreme ambiguity or user-local context requiring clarification), do NOT force a misleading retrieval query. In that case, set `query` to the single short token BRIDGE_UNRECOVERABLE (all caps, no punctuation) so downstream steps can prompt the user for clarification. Do not invent facts.

Query construction rules
- Output exactly one compact line of text as `query`. Keep it short and retrieval-focused (few keywords, no full sentences). Use visible anchors from `question` and `summary_1` and minimal retrieval keywords that express the missing relation (e.g., anchor1 anchor2 which artist recorded both; AlbumTitle album artist genre; 1991–92 NHL season regular season).
- Do not include the final answer, assertions, or user-facing instructions in the `query` field. Avoid polite phrasing, questions to the user, or explanatory text. Prefer noun phrases and relation keywords.
- If your natural impulse would be to write a declarative answer or a clarification sentence in `query`, instead convert that output into a compact retrieval-intent query that targets the missing bridge.
- Preserve semantic fidelity: base the query only on visible anchors and relations in `question` and `summary_1`. Do not invent hidden identifiers or assume unseen facts.

Format contract
- Return exactly one field named `query` whose value is a single short string. If unrecoverable as described above, set `query` to BRIDGE_UNRECOVERABLE.

Examples of disallowed outputs for `query`
- A declarative answer (e.g., "The artist was X.")
- A user-facing clarification sentence (e.g., "Do you mean X or Y?") unless summary_1 explicitly requires clarification — in that case use BRIDGE_UNRECOVERABLE.

Behavioral constraints
- Do not use surface heuristics (token count, punctuation, or quotation marks) as the trigger; apply the semantic rule above.
- Emit only one compact retrieval string when the bridge is missing; do not emit multiple queries or long explanations.

Inputs available: `question`, `summary_1`. Output required: single field `query`.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`. The `summary` must be a concise, post-retrieval integration of the evidence that preserves the facts needed by the final answer. Follow these rules when composing `summary`:

- Purpose: Integrate the first-hop `context` with the newly retrieved `passages` into a grounded state that retains or resolves the earlier bridge only when the second-hop passages supply relation- or attribute-facts that are necessary to answer or disambiguate the `question` (for example: partnership/membership links, joint-award partners indicated by 'with', or requested biographical qualifiers such as nationality, birthdate, current team/status).

- When to append supporting sentence(s): Only append short passage-grounded supporting sentence(s) when the question's semantic intent requires relation or attribute facts to answer or disambiguate. In those cases, produce a compact integrated summary that (a) preserves the concise primary answer or canonical label/token chosen from `context`, and (b) appends one short supporting sentence per relevant entity (or one joint sentence for explicit partnerships) that is directly supported by the supplied `passages` and identity-aligned to the entity referenced in `context`.

- When not to append: Do not append supporting sentences and instead return only the canonical label/token when any of the following apply: the question expects a normalized identifier or single token (examples: binary/multiple-choice label selection between named options, identification-only questions, or explicit token-extraction such as 'middle name'); or the `context` already contains a full, passage-consistent, multi-clause integrated answer. These are semantic exceptions — do not append merely because an answer in `context` is short.

- Identity alignment and verification: Before appending, confirm that the fact in `passages` refers to the same entity referenced in `context` (match names, common aliases, roles, dates, or other corroborating attributes). If identity is ambiguous or unsupported, refrain from appending and preserve uncertainty instead.

- Style and content constraints: Keep output concise. If appending, limit `summary` to 1–3 sentences total (the primary concise answer plus at most one short supporting sentence per relevant entity or one joint supporting sentence). Avoid inventing facts, avoid long verbatim quotations from `passages`, and avoid name-collision errors. Do not change which label or name is selected by the existing answer-selection logic.

- If there is insufficient information in `context` and `passages` to resolve the needed relation/attribute, summarize the uncertainty succinctly rather than asserting unsupported facts.

Produce only the `summary` field as the integrated output string.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Use only summary_1 and summary_2 as evidence. Treat summary_2 as the later, integrated evidence and prioritize it when reconciling information; use summary_1 only as the earlier, pre-second-hop context. Do not assume the final state is unresolved solely because summary_1 contains bridging or retrieval-oriented language.

Produce exactly one field, answer, whose value is the minimal final answer string that is fully supported by the visible summaries. The answer field must contain only that minimal plain-text string and nothing else (no explanation, no surrounding quotes, no JSON wrappers, no debugging or transport artifacts).

Decide whether the question's intent is binary (a truth-value / yes-no question) or non-binary (asking for a name, title, date, place, numeric count, selection, or other factual value). Make this decision semantically (based on the question's meaning), not by surface heuristics like token counts or punctuation.

- If the question's intent is binary AND both summary_1 and summary_2 unambiguously agree on the same truth-value, emit exactly one lowercase token: either yes or no (and nothing else) as the answer value.

- For all other question intents (non-binary), emit the minimal factual string supported by the summaries exactly as needed: preserve multi-token values, necessary punctuation, full date formats, and place formats (for example, full names, "City, State", or "Month Day, Year"), and do not truncate these to a single token.

If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, or do not jointly support an unambiguous minimal answer, do not fabricate or over-assert; follow the system's established fallback behavior for ambiguous or unsupported questions.

Always forbid emitting serialized queries, JSON/dict-like structures, internal-debug wrappers, or other transport artifacts as the final answer. The answer must be plain text only.

Make all judgments about minimality and agreement based on the semantic content of summary_1 and summary_2, with priority to summary_2 when they differ.

Produce the answer value exactly as specified above in the answer field.

response.hop1_query: When was the army whose soldiers Rameshwar Banerjee was trying to get released when he was shot formed?
hop1_docs: Rameshwar Banerjee | Rameshwar Banerjee (Bengali: রামেশ্বর বন্দ্যোপাধ্যায় ; 8 February, 1925 - 21 November, 1945) was a revolutionary and martyr of the Indian Independence Movement.  He participated in the Quit India Movement in 1942.  During the rally organized by the students against British rule and for release of the officers of Indian National Army, he was killed by police firing.

La Sciantosa | "La Sciantosa" is an Italian TV movie produced by RAI television.  (Even though, it was filmed in color, back in 1970, RAI's shows were still being shown in black and white, since they didn't convert to color TV until 1977.)  Anna Magnani played a faded "sciantosa" (i.e., variety show diva), Flora, whose career has declined.  When she receives an invitation to sing at an army base, she thinks she's singing to the front line soldiers, so she accepts the invitation.  She and her assistant, Cristina, played by Rosita Pisano, are met at the train station by Tonino, a young soldier, played by Massimo Ranieri.  He then drives the ladies to the army base, and then introduces them to his misfit band of musicians, including a very annoying horn player, who almost causes Flora not to want to sing for the soldiers, and a guitar player, who ruins a march song by blowing a kazoo at the end.  Flora's set to take the stage, draped in an Italian flag and wearing a crown.  But when the curtain opens, and she sees the severity of some of the soldiers' injuries, she gets instant stage fright.  After 2 unsuccessful tries to get her to do the march song, Tonino tells the band to play "'O surdato 'nnammurato", which Anna Magnani beautifully sings the song herself.  At that point, she has an epiphany and breaks down.  After the performance, the base is under attack, and Tonino helps Flora out of the building.  Unfortunately, Flora and Cristina get separated, and towards the end of the movie, Flora realizes how much she mistreated Cristina, and decides to sacrifice herself by shielding Tonino from an enemy plane firing at his car.

Mickey's Garden | Mickey's Garden is a 1935 American animated short film produced by Walt Disney Productions and released by United Artists.  The film was the second "Mickey Mouse" cartoon shot in Technicolor (after "The Band Concert") and is also the first color appearance of Pluto.  The cartoon is also the first color cartoon where Mickey speaks (though he only does so at the end when Pluto starts licking him).  The film's plot centers on Mickey Mouse trying to rid his garden of insects, but they keep coming back.  When he accidentally gets hit with his own bug spray, he begins seeing a warped reality.

Bloody Sunday (1972) | Bloody Sunday – sometimes called the Bogside Massacre – was an incident on 30 January 1972 in the Bogside area of Derry, Northern Ireland, when British soldiers shot 28 unarmed civilians during a peaceful protest march against internment.  Fourteen people died: thirteen were killed outright, while the death of another man four months later was attributed to his injuries.  Many of the victims were shot while fleeing from the soldiers and some were shot while trying to help the wounded.  Other protesters were injured by rubber bullets or batons, and two were run down by army vehicles.  The march had been organised by the Northern Ireland Civil Rights Association (NICRA).  The soldiers involved were members of the 1st Battalion, Parachute Regiment, also known as "1 Para".

The Recollections of Rifleman Harris | The Recollections of Rifleman Harris is a memoir published in 1848 of the experiences of an enlisted soldier in the 95th Regiment of Foot in the British Army during the Napoleonic Wars.  The eponymous soldier was Benjamin Randell Harris, a private who joined the regiment in 1803 and served in many of the early campaigns in the Peninsula War.  In the mid-1830s, Harris was working as a cobbler in London when he met an acquaintance, Captain Henry Curling, who asked him to dictate an account of his experiences of army life.  This account was then held by Curling until 1848, when he succeeded in getting the manuscript published, preserving one of the very few surviving accounts of military service in this era from a private soldier.

Sander Thoenes | Sander Thoenes (November 7, 1968 – September 21, 1999) was a Dutch journalist who was killed, near Dili in East Timor, by soldiers of Indonesian army.  He was shot when their paths crossed on a road as the Indonesians withdrew from the territory.

Thomas Hickey (soldier) | Thomas Hickey (hanged on June 28, 1776) was a Continental Army soldier in the American Revolutionary War, and the first person executed for treason against what would become the United States.  Born in Ireland, he came to America as a soldier in the British Army and fought as personal assistant to Major General William Johnson in the Seven Years' War, but deserted to the other side when the Revolution broke out.  He became part of the Life Guard, which protected Gen. George Washington, his staff and the Continental Army's payroll.  Hickey was jailed for passing counterfeit money in New York, tried and executed for mutiny and sedition, and he may have been involved in an assassination plot against George Washington in 1776.
response.summary_1:
- evidence:
  - Indian National Army — mentioned in "Rameshwar Banerjee" as "for release of the officers of Indian National Army" (passage [1]).
  - No other candidate army is mentioned in the provided passages.

- ambiguities_or_missing:
  - No ambiguity about which army is referenced (Indian National Army). The passages do not provide its formation date.

- missing_facts_or_bridge:
  - Exact formation date or year of the Indian National Army (when the INA was formed).

- retrieval_direction (prioritized):
  1. "Indian National Army" — main Wikipedia page or authoritative history entry (expects formation date).
  2. "Formation of the Indian National Army Mohan Singh 1942" — page on INA's formation under Mohan Singh / timeline.
  3. "Subhas Chandra Bose Indian National Army formation" — for alternative founding/reorganization date(s).
hop2_docs: Army Ordnance Corps (India) | The Army Ordnance Corps (abbreviated as AOC) is an active corps of the Indian Army and a major formation responsible for providing material and logistical support to the Indian Army during war and peace.  The history of the Ordnance Corps dates back to the 18th century, which makes it one of the oldest formations of the Indian Army.

Washington Army National Guard | The Washington Army National Guard is a component of the United States Army and the Washington National Guard based in Washington.  The history of the Washington Army National Guard dates back to 1854 with formation of the Washington Territorial Militia.  The command is headquartered at Camp Murray in Pierce County.  It consists of 6,200 soldiers in two brigades and various smaller units located throughout the state.

K. P. K. Menon | K. P. Keshava Menon (born 1884?)  was an Indian lawyer and a leading Indian independence activist from Kerala who was a key proponent of the formation of the Indian Independence League (IIL) and a lawyer for the Indian National Army (INA).

50th Indian Tank Brigade | The 50th Indian Tank Brigade was an armoured brigade formation of the Indian Army during World War II.  It was formed for service in the Burma Campaign of World War II from units of the British Army and the British Indian Army.  The brigade's formation emblem was a white upraised fist and forearm on a black disc.

Farrer Park address | The Farrer Park address was an assembly of the surrendered Indian troops of the British Indian Army held at Farrer Park in Singapore on 17 February 1942, two days after the Fall of Singapore.  The assembly was marked by a series of three addresses in which the British Malaya Command formally surrendered the Indian troops of the British Indian Army to Major Fujiwara Iwaichi representing the Japanese military authority, followed by transfer of authority by Fujiwara to the command of Mohan Singh, and a subsequent address by Mohan Singh to the gathered troops declaring the formation of the Indian National Army to fight the "Raj", asking for volunteers to join the army.
Indian Meadows Formation | The Indian Meadows Formation is a geologic formation in Wyoming.  It preserves fossils dating back to the Paleogene period.
Indian Point Formation | The Indian Point Formation is a geologic formation in Quebec.  It preserves fossils dating back to the Silurian period.
response.summary_2: 17 February 1942 — the Indian National Army was declared formed at the Farrer Park address by Mohan Singh on 17 February 1942.
